title: Tool Reviews
text:
Factiva could be a great place to hit first when researching a company, but may be less useful the deeper your research project goes and the more private the company happens to be. The company snapshot looks familiar because it is using the same data that many other tools use. However, I appreciate that Factiva often includes its source in any given module. Its weaknesses are shared by many other platforms that rely on data brokers: public data just rerendered, not necessarily any original research being done on the platform, which means many other services have the same exact data. Factivity’s main strength is its simplicity.



Company Snapshot
News

Latest News
Web News

Signals
Key Developments
Peer Comparison

Lots of options here, with spreadsheet export
You can see similar companies based on sales, employee count, or market cap
Potentially useful when brainstorming proactive research, since you can see the top 10 most similar companies, the names attached to those companies may also be relevant to your project.

Corporate Family

One of the best features, in my opinion, very simple rendering of Dun & Bradstreet information but with some nice additional touches
They have developed different symbols to indicate parent vs. primary subsidiary vs. regular subsidiary vs. branch location
You can see all branch and subsidiary names and locations

Financial Results

Most of this category is not relevant for prospect research,

Ownership

Instead of digging through SEC filings to find insiders who own stock, Factiva has the top 10 within a couple clicks, which could save time

Analysis and Profiles

Geared more toward investment advice, but provides some useful or interesting context about a company when you look at the most recent financials. The Wright Quality rating, for example, gives you an at-a-glance letter grade to consider when evaluating the company’s charitability, and could be a useful small infographic to include in a report from a prospect researcher.

Reports

The “company report” stitches together almost all of the individual pieces into one PDF or html, and seems like the most comprehensive, but most is potentially irrelevant to a prospect research

Chrome/Edge Browser Extension

Haven’t tested it yet, but it functions as an additional way to search during your workflow






3-source verify: Factiva is a quick place to verify public information about companies, especially the relationship between parent or holding companies and their subsidiaries.
Wrangling Corporate Sprawl: When deep diving in on companies, mapping out the parents and subsidiaries is only the beginning. Factiva provides a concise, sourced, and reliable source of industry information that can help understand big companies and their so-called sprawl sometimes across several markets.
C-Suite Searching: In the subjects on the right side of the News module, you can click “Senior Level Management” and it will bring up the latest news regarding senior management. One of the unique sources there: “The Official Board” which is sometimes behind a pay wall

PitchBook is a financial data platform used by university libraries to provide detailed information on venture capital, private equity, and merger and acquisition activity across the private and public markets.






ProQuest


ProQuest is a global information-content and technology company that provides academic libraries and researchers with access to a massive collection of digitized dissertations, historical newspapers, scholarly journals, and primary source documents.

title: Using PowerQuery
text:
Power Query is a data connection technology in Excel that allows users to connect, combine, and refine data from various sources.



Data Importing: Pulls data from multiple sources (databases, web pages, files).
Data Transformation: Offers tools to clean and reshape data, such as filtering, merging, and aggregating.
User-Friendly Interface: Provides a visual interface for users to apply transformations without coding.
Integration: Seamlessly integrates with Excel, allowing transformed data to be loaded into worksheets for analysis.

In essence, Power Query simplifies data preparation, enabling users to focus on analysis and insights.

title: Using PowerQuery
text:
Power Query is a data connection technology in Excel that allows users to connect, combine, and refine data from various sources.



Data Importing: Pulls data from multiple sources (databases, web pages, files).
Data Transformation: Offers tools to clean and reshape data, such as filtering, merging, and aggregating.
User-Friendly Interface: Provides a visual interface for users to apply transformations without coding.
Integration: Seamlessly integrates with Excel, allowing transformed data to be loaded into worksheets for analysis.

In essence, Power Query simplifies data preparation, enabling users to focus on analysis and insights.

title: Using PowerQuery
text:
PowerQuery vs. VLookup








Feature
Power Query
VLOOKUP




Purpose
Data transformation and preparation
Look up and retrieve specific data values


Data Sources
Connects to multiple sources (Excel, SQL, web, etc.)
Limited to data within the worksheet


Complexity
More complex with advanced capabilities
Simpler and straightforward


Output
Creates tables or queries in Excel
Returns a single value based on a match


Flexibility
Highly versatile, allows for data shaping
Less flexible; limited to a single match type


Use Cases
Ideal for ETL processes and bulk data manipulation
Best for simple lookups within a single dataset


Maintenance
Requires more setup but easier to update over time
Simple to set up but can be error-prone if data changes

title: Using PowerQuery
text:
Use-cases
Your workflow may not require PowerQuery, it may be overkill. But given how much fiddling with Excel formulas can happen, and how that can eat up time, PowerQuery honestly seems like it should be more standard in our field - especially because of how reproducible any given process is - the steps being recorded automatically so they could be reused or made into a template.

Abstrqct Example
You have data in your CRM that you want to combine with another dataset you’ve found or created. For example, perhaps you’ve created a custom list of individuals who have won awards, but have not updated each of their individual profiles in the database yet. So you are looking to add this supplemental info to help segment a list in an interesting way.


Steps to Achieve This

Export Data from CRM: Start by exporting the relevant data from your CRM into a CSV or Excel format.
Import Data into Power Query: Open Excel, go to the Data tab, and select “Get Data” to import your CRM data and your custom dataset.
Combine Datasets: Use Power Query tools to merge or append the datasets based on a common identifier.
Transform Data: Clean, filter, or reshape the data as necessary.
Load the Combined Data: Once satisfied with the combined data, load it back into Excel for analysis or reporting.

More complex data engineering can be done with PowerQuery, and business intelligence analytics with PowerBI

title: Using PowerQuery
section: Power Query M Code for Filtering Job Titles with Dynamic Keywords
text:
Power Query M Code for Filtering Job Titles with Dynamic Keywords
This document outlines a Power Query M script that dynamically loads keyword lists from parameters and filters job title datasets across multiple sectors. The goal is to identify roles related to Major Gifts while excluding irrelevant ones.

Dynamic Keyword Parameters
Instead of hardcoding the keywords, we define them as parameters:
let
    IncludeKeywords = Text.Split("Major Gift,Director Of Development,Development Officer,Major Gift Officer,Fundraiser,Philanthropy Officer,Philanthropy Manager", ","),
    ExcludeKeywords = Text.Split("Prospect Research,Event", ",")


Load and Filter Nonprofit Dataset
We load the nonprofit dataset and filter it using the dynamic keyword lists:
    Source = Csv.Document(File.Contents("..\jobtitlesdataset\Figure 4\dataset\titles\map\55198385_nonprofit.csv"), [Delimiter=",", Columns=7, Encoding=65001, QuoteStyle=QuoteStyle.None]),
    PromotedHeaders = Table.PromoteHeaders(Source, [PromoteAllScalars=true]),
    ChangedType = Table.TransformColumnTypes(PromotedHeaders, {
        {"sector", type text}, 
        {"title_cleaned", type text}, 
        {"frequency_cleaned", Int64.Type}, 
        {"title_generalized", type text}, 
        {"frequency_generalized", Int64.Type}, 
        {"title_simplified", type text}, 
        {"frequency_simplified", Int64.Type}
    }),
    IncludedRows = Table.SelectRows(ChangedType, each List.AnyTrue(List.Transform(IncludeKeywords, (kw) => Text.Contains([title_cleaned], kw)))),
    FinalFilteredRows = Table.SelectRows(IncludedRows, each List.AllTrue(List.Transform(ExcludeKeywords, (kw) => not Text.Contains([title_cleaned], kw)))),
    TitleList = Table.SelectColumns(FinalFilteredRows, {"title_cleaned"}),


Reusable Filtering Function
We define a reusable function to apply the same filtering logic to other datasets:
    FilterDataset = (filePath as text) =>
        let
            Source = Csv.Document(File.Contents(filePath), [Delimiter=",", Columns=7, Encoding=65001, QuoteStyle=QuoteStyle.None]),
            Promoted = Table.PromoteHeaders(Source, [PromoteAllScalars=true]),
            ChangedType = Table.TransformColumnTypes(Promoted, {
                {"sector", type text}, 
                {"title_cleaned", type text}, 
                {"frequency_cleaned", Int64.Type}, 
                {"title_generalized", type text}, 
                {"frequency_generalized", Int64.Type}, 
                {"title_simplified", type text}, 
                {"frequency_simplified", Int64.Type}
            }),
            Matched = Table.Join(ChangedType, "title_cleaned", TitleList, "title_cleaned", JoinKind.Inner)
        in
            Matched,


Dataset Paths and Execution
We list all dataset paths and apply the filtering function:
    DatasetPaths = {
        "..\jobtitlesdataset\Figure 4\dataset\titles\map\55198352_travel_and_tourism.csv",
        "..\jobtitlesdataset\Figure 4\dataset\titles\map\55198376_transportation_and_logistics.csv",
        "..\jobtitlesdataset\Figure 4\dataset\titles\map\55197857_restaurants_bars_and_food_services.csv",
        "..\jobtitlesdataset\Figure 4\dataset\titles\map\55198349_real_estate.csv",
        "..\jobtitlesdataset\Figure 4\dataset\titles\map\55198379_telecommunications.csv",
        "..\jobtitlesdataset\Figure 4\dataset\titles\map\55198388_oil_gas_energy_and_utilities.csv",
        "..\jobtitlesdataset\Figure 4\dataset\titles\map\55198394_retail.csv",
        "..\jobtitlesdataset\Figure 4\dataset\titles\map\55198373_media.csv",
        "..\jobtitlesdataset\Figure 4\dataset\titles\map\55198403_manufacturing.csv",
        "..\jobtitlesdataset\Figure 4\dataset\titles\map\55198358_insurance.csv",
        "..\jobtitlesdataset\Figure 4\dataset\titles\map\55198406_information_technology.csv",
        "..\jobtitlesdataset\Figure 4\dataset\titles\map\55198382_government.csv",
        "..\jobtitlesdataset\Figure 4\dataset\titles\map\55198397_health_care.csv",
        "..\jobtitlesdataset\Figure 4\dataset\titles\map\55198391_education.csv",
        "..\jobtitlesdataset\Figure 4\dataset\titles\map\55198400_finance.csv",
        "..\jobtitlesdataset\Figure 4\dataset\titles\map\55198190_consumer_services.csv",
        "..\jobtitlesdataset\Figure 4\dataset\titles\map\55198364_construction_repair_and_maintenance.csv",
        "..\jobtitlesdataset\Figure 4\dataset\titles\map\55198409_business_services.csv",
        "..\jobtitlesdataset\Figure 4\dataset\titles\map\55197803_agriculture_and_forestry.csv",
        "..\jobtitlesdataset\Figure 4\dataset\titles\map\55198355_arts_entertainment_and_recreation.csv",
        "..\jobtitlesdataset\Figure 4\dataset\titles\map\55198370_biotech_and_pharmaceuticals.csv",
        "..\jobtitlesdataset\Figure 4\dataset\titles\map\55198361_aerospace_and_defense.csv",
        "..\jobtitlesdataset\Figure 4\dataset\titles\map\55198367_accounting_and_legal.csv"
    },
    FilteredTables = List.Transform(DatasetPaths, each FilterDataset(_)),
    CombinedResults = Table.Combine(FilteredTables)
in
    CombinedResults


Summary
This script dynamically loads keyword lists and applies consistent filtering across multiple datasets. It improves maintainability and scalability by avoiding hardcoded values.

title: Tool & Company Incidents
text:
Incidents include events like data breaches, service outages, and any press about abandoned features. This website gathers ad hoc data reported publicly online, focused primarily on fundraising software relating to prospect research. As far as I’m aware, this is the first repository of fundraising software niche information of its kind - intended to be a transparent and independent collation of incidents for research purposes. These incidents contribute to the rating of any given software tool, with more severe incidents having a bigger impact on the score. The methodology for the tool ratings will be published in a different blog post.
The goal is to make this incident reporting useful especially for shops that are evaluating new tools, so they can make an educated decision on where they place their budgetary resources.

title: Tool & Company Incidents
text:
Data breaches
…

title: Tool & Company Incidents
text:
Service Outages
…

title: Tool & Company Incidents
text:
Feature Timeline issues
…

title: Tool & Company Incidents
text:
Privacy and Transparency
Donors also have a right to know about the logistics of fundraising, in detail, so they can make decisions about where they place their money and more to the point: their information. A foundation’s choice of software is relevant to the overall mission of philanthropy, and is part of the ongoing conversation about how fundraising resources are deployed in service of the institution’s mission.
For some countries, like those in the European Union, there are laws that help protect citizens from incidents like data breaches. However, this Prospect Research Toolkit is focused on the USA, where the laws vary by state, and tech companies are given wide leeway to innovate - occasionally at the cost of user safety.
The tech world moves fast, and the engineers are not always on the same page as their front line sales people. Rapid changes in the industry can alter the feature catalog, the data storage, structure, and dissemination practices of any given tool. It would be an impossible and tangential task for any given prospect dev shop to try and keep up with it all on their own.
Similarly, fundraising institutions do not share pricing information with each other and many fundraising-niche software companies are highly secretive about pricing tiers - preferring to negotiate with clients individually. However, I believe that transparency can actually drive prices down while improving quality of service - which is why these incidents are carefully evaluated to make sure they are accurate.

title: Library Databases for Prospect Dev Analysts
text:
University libraries often pay for access to powerful databases and research platforms—many of which are underutilized by fundraising and advancement teams. These resources can provide valuable insights for prospect research, verification, and strategic decision-making. This post explores how we can better leverage these tools, understand their data sources, and even automate workflows to make research more efficient.



Identifying potential tools is fairly easy. The real challenge lies in defining use cases and determining how to export and correlate information from different systems. This process also helps us understand our data sources better. For example, many platforms—such as iWave, Factiva, and others—rely on Dun & Bradstreet data when compiling reports. Factiva even publishes a monthly update listing new sources, with a total of 30,000+ sources available.




A detailed list of tools gives us more confidence when verifying research. The reliability of any piece of information increases when it appears across multiple sources (e.g., two independent news articles confirming a job title). These tools can be great for spot checks when confirming details.




Most platforms aggregate data from large brokerages, but each has its own way of processing and presenting that information. For example, iWave uses proprietary techniques to build profiles. While these methods likely follow standard data science practices, the exact process is often unclear—this is known as the “black box” problem.
We can reduce this uncertainty by:

Identifying as many sources as possible.
Understanding the data brokers behind those sources.
Drawing conclusions about how platforms transform raw data.

This knowledge adds confidence to the reports we share internally and helps us explain where information originates—even if some steps remain opaque.




Some tools specialize in niche areas. For example, Pitchbook focuses on venture capital and startup funding rounds. This project may reveal which tools are best suited for specific research problems.




Comparing results across multiple sources is a common practice for prospect researchers. Doing this manually across 20+ tools can be time-consuming. In OSINT (open-source intelligence), developers have created tools that search hundreds of sources simultaneously and categorize results. However, most OSINT tools aren’t ethical for prospect research because they rely on breach data or are designed for law enforcement.
Our use case is different, so we’d need to build our own multi-channel search solution or support existing efforts like the Prospect Research Toolkit. Possible approaches include:

Power Automate Desktop: Create a flow that prompts for a search term and runs searches across selected channels (e.g., Privco, Mergent Online).
Power Query Integration: Automate data export and correlation for comprehensive reports. For example, if we have a list of names for an event, automation could pull data from multiple databases and compile it into a spreadsheet with columns for each source.

Companies like Xapien already do advanced versions of this using custom algorithms and web scraping to verify information.




We’re building a spreadsheet with links and feature comparisons for all tools under review. This will serve as a reference for choosing the right tool for the right research problem.

Disclaimer - This text was either summarized, re-organized, or partially generated by AI.

title: About
text:
The prospect research toolkit is an independently maintained repository of fundraising industry analysis, software information, and research methodology. This is a hobby coding project, first and foremost, but projects are refined based on conversations with development professionals.
There are plenty of excellent paid resources already available: Apra, private consultant firms, subscription tools, books…but not everyone has the budget to access them. This toolkit was created to bridge that gap, or supplement what’s already out there, and create original content that is interactive. The goal is to keep it simple, fun, open-source, community-driven, regularly updated, and always free.

title: About
text:
How accurate is this website?
There will be an entire write-up explaining how I source pricing information, but generally speaking, the prices on this website will be prone to errors and should be understood as estimates. The incidents, however, given their public nature, are a more reliable metric to contribute to the overall rating scheme of the software tools.
In order of most to least accuracy (probably):

General tool, feature, or company information
Tutorials
Pricing information

title: About
text:
Who is this guy anyway?
I come from a psychology background and am largely self-taught in data analytics, information science, data science, etc. This website is guaranteed to have some bugs, or occasionally be broken.

title: About
section: How often is the website updated?
text:
How often is the website updated?
In small ways, frequently! You can read the latest release notes for some of the technical details.

title: Overview: Data Flow and Actors
text:
From ChatGPT:
Here is the same breakdown of the relationship between data brokers, financial intelligence services, and niche fundraising tools — with a References section including plaintext URLs at the end so you can follow the sources yourself.

title: Overview: Data Flow and Actors
text:
1. Data Brokers
What they are: Data brokers collect, clean, link, standardize, and resell data about people and businesses to third parties. They pull from public records, commercial databases, and other sources and package the information for clients. (Wikipedia)
What they provide:

Aggregated datasets
Identity attributes
Employment histories
Wealth proxies
Proprietary linkages used for analytics Clients include marketers, compliance teams, financial institutions, and software vendors. (Wikipedia)

Academic perspective: Studies on data marketplaces show how brokers function as intermediaries in commoditized information markets, pricing and trading data between owners and consumers. (arXiv)

title: Overview: Data Flow and Actors
section: 2. Financial Intelligence Services (FIUs & Private FININT Vendors)
text:
2. Financial Intelligence Services (FIUs & Private FININT Vendors)
What they are: Financial intelligence services include government Financial Intelligence Units (FIUs) and private FININT vendors that analyze data for purposes such as anti–money-laundering (AML), sanctions screening, risk assessment, and compliance.
How they use data: They ingest:

Transaction records
Watchlists
Proprietary and open source intelligence
Broker data

to produce actionable intelligence. Open-source intelligence (OSINT) — including public records and some broker data — is explicitly used to support investigations. (Egmont Group)
FIUs and OSINT: The Egmont Group, which coordinates global FIU collaboration, reports that OSINT contributes to operational and strategic analysis by helping identify links, patterns, and risks not visible in transaction data alone. (Egmont Group)

title: Overview: Data Flow and Actors
text:
3. Niche Fundraising Tools (e.g., ResearchPoint & LiveAlumni)
These products are end-user applications built for nonprofit development, advancement, and alumni relations. They consume data from brokers and other sources to help fundraising professionals prioritize prospects and manage engagement.

Blackbaud ResearchPoint

A donor prospect research platform that integrates multiple data feeds (including wealth, philanthropic history, biographical, and asset data). (Blackbaud Help)
It supports bulk lists, wealth screening, and rich prospect profiles used to guide major gift fundraising. (Blackbaud Help)



LiveAlumni

A niche provider focused on verified alumni and donor employment data, job changes, industry and employer details. (LiveAlumni)
Useful in fundraising to identify job changes, matching gift eligibility, and engagement signals.

These platforms often license or embed multiple broker data feeds to enrich constituent records and analytics.

title: Helpful Technical Documentation
text: This list is mostly for Greg as he maintains this website.

title: Helpful Technical Documentation
text:
Quarto guides

Practical Quarto
Quarto presentations

title: Helpful Technical Documentation
text:
Website Maintenance

Use the drafts feature to preview and plan content
Learn how to use the profiles feature
Prevent all unnecessary folders and files from rendering to the website (how?)
learn incremental render

Yes, you can preview .qmd files inside an excluded folder like scripts/ without moving them, but it requires a bit of manual action because Quarto won’t render them automatically during your site build.
Here’s how to do it:

title: Helpful Technical Documentation
section: How to preview .qmd files in excluded folders without moving them
text:
How to preview .qmd files in excluded folders without moving them

1. Render them manually, one at a time
From your project root, run:
quarto render scripts/your_file.qmd

This renders just that file (ignores your site’s render exclusion)
Output goes to _output folder by default (not your website’s docs/)
You can open the generated HTML in your browser to preview




2. Use the quarto preview command for that file
quarto preview scripts/your_file.qmd

Opens a local preview server with live reload for that file
Handy for iterative editing and testing




3. Use VS Code Quarto extension to preview
If you have the Quarto extension in VS Code:

Open scripts/your_file.qmd
Use “Preview” button in editor (usually top right or right-click menu)
This previews the current file regardless of site config




4. Temporarily disable the exclusion (if you want full site preview)
Remove or comment out the render: - "!scripts/**" line

Run quarto render to include those files
Preview site with those pages included
Re-enable exclusion when done

title: Helpful Technical Documentation
text:
Summary








Method
Pros
Cons




quarto render file.qmd
Fast, manual render
Manual, one file at a time


quarto preview file.qmd
Live reload, interactive
One file only


VS Code Quarto preview
Integrated, easy
Requires VS Code & extension


Disable exclusion temporarily
Full site preview
Changes site config temporarily

editable reveal js lets you do some basic editing within revealjs presentations themselves, and then save it back to your QMD, which makes for a nice workflow similar to working in powerpoint


Visualization Extensions

Diagrams
Svelte components



Article Interactivity

Lightboxes
expanding “nutshell” notes so people don’t have to switch tabs



Embedding

social media embeds, easy to embed YouTube
PDF embedding



User Experience

downloading embedded data or images
list of links at end of page
Quarto badges, lets you make fun stuff like this!

Example Quarto Sites

good example of webr and download capabilities

title: UVU Philanthropy Workshop
text:
Welcome to the workshop!


all the long, rambling bullet points need to be cut down to 5-10 words, put any other useful context in the speaker notes. - Add more questions to the audience. If possible, practice this out loud to see how it stacks up against the 20 minute slot.
Add more formatting or interesting stuff (e.g. images) to the presentation itself, right now, it’s all text
Some Quarto extensions could add more engaging elements (e.g. nutshell)
Maybe make the ugly Google Sheet of prospect dev jobs available as a public link? Until the job board on the site is live?

title: UVU Philanthropy Workshop
text:
Greg’s Background

BS in Psychology
Prospect research analyst, but more on that later
Early-career fundraising professional
VP at Apra Utah, a local professional org for those in my niche
Former SIMLab contributer
Former Impact Evaluation Fellow at the Center for Social Impact



Yes, you can connect with me on LinkedIn (I try to post about open jobs in my field, especially in Utah)
GO THROUGH AND MAKE ALL BULLET POINTS SHORTER, PUT LONGER STUFF IN SPEAKER NOTES
Psych BS: degree which I loved, but of the many classes I took, I think some of my research methods classes were the most relevant. I switched majors so much it took me ~8 years to finally finish. Don’t do what I did, which was essentially spend a lot of money to learn that I don’t want to be a therapist, and that running a peer support group is more than enough to occupy that impulse.
I was a part-time student for most of my undergraduate studies, for financial reasons, until a bipolar scholarship got me more seriously into finishing school.
Tangent: That bipolar scholarship is a fascinating example of philanthropy, because it acknowledged how bipolar can affect your ability to graduate, complete advanced courses, etc. Not many people, including the Accessibility Office at UVU, when I went to them for help years ago, actually understand that lived experience and the accomodations that may help. (Baer Reintegration Scholarship). For example: it didn’t require me to be a freshman, it allowed for a wide variety of paths, like cosmetology school, and crucially, it allowed for part-time schooling. Arguably one of the biggest gaps in our current scholarship environment are part-time scholarships, for reasons I may mention later on, but the gist of it is: major donors do not understand their value, they seem less prestigious or rigorous, they may not understand the financial situation of the average student, or they are just relying on the fundraisers and program directors for the details of the scholarship, and they default to full-time for a myriad of reasons. Unfortunately, for many things, if it looks weird to a donor, they won’t fund it, and as you can imagine, the experience of a lower income student vs. a high dollar philanthropist is so disparate, that relying solely on the donor’s judgement for those details is a flawed and biased way of structuring the philanthropy
My professional background colors my opinion on philanthropy, and may make me a touch more skeptical in my tone, but that’s because I believe philanthropy is surprisingly difficult to do well from a social impact perspective: if we are trying to do no harm to ourselves, interpersonally, communally, and at a broader cultural level. But a bit more on that later
Worked as an analyst for UVU’s “advancement” division (industry jargon for higher-ed fundraising), now at the University of Utah doing the same thing (though with more zeros involved with all the numbers I look at)
SIMLab: contributed to Project READ analysis, the social impact evaluation primer, and an analysis of the impact of college counselors on student performance. Didn’t make any waves, and I was arguably the dumbest guy in a room with a highly trained economist, highly competent student coders, etc. When you can, try to get into the rooms where everyone else has more skills than you do, it makes for great ambient learning and personal development.
Easily my favorite college experience, and I think plenty of workplaces could learn important lessons about teamwork culture from what they do here at the center
I haven’t done any public speaking in a while, because my day-to-day life involves sitting at a desk in a basement muttering curse words because ResearchPoint (a fundraising database) is suffering from another service outage, so I’m attempting to make this interesting, and failing that, useful for understanding opportunites in my career path, and in fundraising as a whole.

title: UVU Philanthropy Workshop
section: Is philanthropy relevant to my life as a student?
text:
Is philanthropy relevant to my life as a student?

Yes, though the average student is not a high-dollar philanthropist (or connected to one via family)
More relevant as the source of student aid (e.g. scholarships)
Philanthropy is connected to industries where a student might find employment (e.g. fundraising)

title: UVU Philanthropy Workshop
text:
What is philanthropy?

Tax efficient PR strategy (individual & corporate)
Strategic means for community or legislative impact
Fodder for a boring slide-deck if I’m not careful



There’s that quote about journalism: journalism is printing something somebody else does not want to be printed, everything else is PR.

title: UVU Philanthropy Workshop
text:
Is philanthropy effective?

In the short term, easy to say yes
Taking the long view, things get complicated
Criticism of philanthropy is a storied tradition
Progress since the Gilded Age? 1



The 2010s saw a slew of increasingly dire book titles, “No such thing as a free gift: The Gates Foundation and the price of philanthropy”, “Just giving: why philanthropy is failing democracy and how it can do better”, “winners take all: the elite charade of changing the world”, “The tyranny of generosity: why philanthropy corrupts our politics and how we can fix it”
criticism and accountability are good things, especially when backed up by data. The truth is always more complicated, of course, but even though I actively work in philanthropy, I certainly agree with many of the broad strokes criticisms
Academics are constantly connecting poverty and lower income to health problems
mention bruce bastian and alan ashton if there is time (word perfect, dueling philanthropy, quote about wallets, etc)
“The U.S. Attorney General at this time, George Wickersham, went so far as to write off philanthropic foundations as “scheme[s] for perpetuating mass wealth” and “entirely inconsistent with the public interest,” while other critics expressed their opinion that philanthropy “was merely a ploy to secure the wealth and clean up the reputations of business moguls who amassed fortunes during the robber baron years” (McGoey, 2015, p. 4; Barkan, 2013, p. 635). Even Theodore Roosevelt opposed Rockefeller’s effort to establish a private foundation, claiming that “no amount of charity in spending such fortunes [as Rockefeller’s] can compensate in any way for the misconduct in acquiring them Scholar Lindsay McGoey lists three main criticisms of philanthrocapitalism: The erosion of support for government spending on health and education due to the channeling of private funds toward public services, the potential lack of accountability and transparency of private philanthropists, and the exacerbation of of socioeconomic inequalities through unethical business strategies that are mainly concerned with maximizing profit (McGoey, 2015, pp. 9-10). he main concern is that the donor gains too much power and influence over how the recipient organization spends its money, reducing the independence of that organization, and affecting the population that it serves. stems from evidence showing that extremely wealthy philanthropists earn their fortunes through business tactics that have “compounded financial instability, eroded labour protections, and entrenched global economic inequalities,” including violating antitrust laws and using their monopoly power to stifle competition- from Big Philanthropy in The United States: Do the Benefits Outweigh the Risks? by Rachel N Clift for her grant writing class at portland state

title: UVU Philanthropy Workshop
text:
tangent…

Rockefeller as King

title: UVU Philanthropy Workshop
text:
Philanthropy at the Student Scale

Less than, say, $1000 or less over ~5 years
The Brooklyn Org example, micro DAF 2
Giving Days at the U example, some mini drives led by students + matching programs
Matching gifts and Microsoft rewards 3



Even more than making a private, small donation - a program/project/non-profit may appreciate you organizing a small drive or campaign on their behalf. Some jobs, for example, will match your donation to a list of approved non-profits, which makes your money stretch farther and have more impact. Your coworkers may not know about the matching program, so organizing a small office drive to take advantage of the matching (which is often limited by time) doesn’t require a bigger donation from you, just a bit more time.
Organizing a small campaign like this is something you can also put on your resume, if you are applying for fundraising positions as a graphic designer, data analyst, event planner, prospect researcher, and more. There are ways to donate money without pulling directly from your wallet: for example, if you use Bing for a few search engine queries every month, you can donate the Microsoft Reward points to many causes: including UVU! When I worked at UVU, I had a job that required me to search online constantly for information, and I ended up raising $50+ for the University’s general fund. Not a huge amount, but I did not have to dip into my own finances for that particular donation.
There are other programs like this out there, and for a student on a budget (vs. a wealthy philanthropist) they are a simple way to contribute smaller one-time gifts to causes you want to support, but can’t justify using your own limited resources.
Brooklyn Org: what’s a DAF? Why is the Brooklyn Org interesting?
due dilligence is exhausting, so sometimes it’s nice to have an org that does it for you

title: UVU Philanthropy Workshop
text:
Macro-trends in Charitable Giving

Small-dollar donor participation is in steep decline
Similarly, total number of donors is going down
Institutional trust frequently shredded by scandal



lower total donors = small group of high-dollar donors everyone is chasing
Your average donor who isn’t a billionaire or multi-millionaire may be more compelled by one-off projects, or local programs, or things that they think have social impact that aren’t explicitly philanthropy
Elon Musk quote that is very revealing: “My businesses are my philanthropy”, as in, contributing to the overall economic output of the country via enterprise translates to downstream benefits for the average person more than giving them money via philanthropic vehicles. Any reactions to that?

title: UVU Philanthropy Workshop
text:
Fundraising career paths

Generally every fundraising role has a for-profit counterpart or close analogue (IT, sales, marketing, accounting, etc)
Public (hospitals, universities, etc)
Private foundations



Like in business, the highest paid professionals tend to be those with a portfolio (in this case, of donors instead of accounts), who bring in revenue, and then the C-suite or upper admin
In my role, I make $60k a year, started off in the mid-40s, full-time, with benefits
Plenty of other operational roles, which deal with varying levels of chaos
somewhat seasonal, circling around events
Many pros and many cons, like with any job, one of the pros for me is that I get to work for a University, and since I find education to be a fundamental social good, some of my personal values are represented in my job
It helps to save a list of keywords that help you filter the job titles down to what you want
Insert some examples, potentially curated based on the majors in the room, or maybe have a list and ask if anyone is interested in zooming in on something specific - maybe the brooklyn org, or UVU advancement

title: UVU Philanthropy Workshop
text:
Prospect Dev

Overview
What’s prospect management?
What’s prospect research?
Why should I care? (finding your niche)



Center for Social Impact education, student jobs, and fellowships is actually great prep for many of the roles I’m highlighting (e.g. you are studying accounting or business, but don’t have fundraising experience yet)
At the University of Utah, where I work, the org chart is a bit more complicated, but similar. Intermountain Health is another good one to look at.
Org charts are a great way to get key words for job searches, especially because sometimes some careers just rename jobs so they can feel fancy (fundraising does this constantly, though it’s not as bad as the tech field)

title: UVU Philanthropy Workshop
text:
How it looks at UVU

Division is called Institutional Advancement
Campaigns are busy times at work
UVU set a goal of $350 million (they hit $250M)



advancement being the fancy higher ed word for fundraising
UVU does a couple odd things with how their org chart has shaken out in its details, but generally you’ll see these roles / teams in other higher ed fundraising teams, and many similarities with Hospital fundraising teams, Nonprofits are highly varied, so they may diverge, but the bigger ones also tend to have similar org charts
VP is in the University President’s Cabinet, one of the lead fundraisers, alongside the President
AVP of Development is the leader of the frontline fundraisers, and is a fundraiser themselves, they lead the development team (at UVU they are called Directors of Development, but job titles vary, like Major Gift Officer), these folks tend to be more extroverted, often working with specific colleges especially alongside the Deans to fund programs, scholarships, etc…and you have several related teams like Annual Giving, Planned Giving, Alumni Relations (if there wasn’t so much event planning, I would personally love to work in this world more), and Prospect Dev (sometimes Prospect Dev is on the data team instead, people sometimes don’t know where it fits)
Senior Director of Foundation Finance and Compliance, runs the endowment, has to host auditors when they drop by (a stressful day), leads the accountants, tries to keep everybody realistic
AVP of Strategic Engagement, leads the Executive Events team, and the Marketing & Communications (Marcom is common jargon there) team: this is where you have your web designers, your graphic designers, content creators, and event planners.
All these leaders often have executive assistants

title: UVU Philanthropy Workshop
section: How it looks in the for-profit world
text:
How it looks in the for-profit world

Many companies have foundations or social impact missions that require operational staff
You will make more money in the for-profit world (e.g. an account manager at a Fortune 500 company instead of a development officer at a college)

title: UVU Philanthropy Workshop
text:
Your values vs. your job

Values may drive many of your career moves
A single year can bring massive changes
Utah culture around “make the money, be a philanthropist later”



and you have to draw your own lines in the sand (e.g. would you work for a Political Action Committee? What kind? Religious orgs are the biggest recipents of charitable donations, many of them need fundraising staff, but what if the one you are working for has a scandal?)
Especially if you don’t work for an institution that has an endowment, those changes can test you
If, like me when I graduated, you just want to land any job that can pay the rent and give you healthcare, it can be difficult to take your values into account if they feel like roadblocks

title: UVU Philanthropy Workshop
text:

Community Foundation of Utah DAFs
Brooklyn Org - DAFs and Community Impact
Funraise’s state of fundraising 2026

title: Internal Data Contracts
text:
As organizations grow, data becomes increasingly complex and distributed across multiple teams and systems. Without clear agreements on how data is structured, maintained, and shared, teams risk miscommunication, broken pipelines, and unreliable analytics. Internal data contracts solve this problem by formalizing expectations between data producers and consumers—similar to API contracts, but for data.
This post will cover: - What internal data contracts are - A sample contract template - Best practices for implementation - How they can apply to fundraising analysis teams




An internal data contract is a documented agreement that defines: - Schema: The structure and types of data fields - Quality guarantees: Rules for completeness, accuracy, and freshness - Ownership: Who is responsible for producing and consuming the data - Access and security: Permissions and compliance requirements - Change management: How updates are communicated and versioned
These contracts reduce breakages, improve trust, and enable scalable data sharing across teams.




Title: Customer Data Contract
Version: 1.0
Owner: Data Engineering Team
Effective Date: 2025-11-12



This contract defines the structure, quality, and governance of the customer dataset shared between the CRM system (producer) and Analytics team (consumer).













Field Name
Data Type
Nullable
Description




customer_id
STRING
No
Unique customer identifier


name
STRING
No
Full name of the customer


email
STRING
No
Customer email address


signup_date
DATE
No
Date of account creation


status
STRING
Yes
Active, Inactive, Pending



Versioning:
- Backward-compatible changes allowed (adding new nullable fields).
- Breaking changes require 30-day notice and version increment.





Freshness: Data updated within 15 minutes of CRM changes.

Completeness: 99% of records must have email populated.

Accuracy: status must match CRM system values.






Producer: CRM Engineering Team

Consumer: Analytics Team

Escalation: Contact data-support@company.com for issues.






Data accessible via internal data warehouse with role-based permissions.

PII fields (e.g., email) must comply with GDPR and internal privacy policies.






All schema changes documented in the internal data catalog.

Breaking changes require approval from both producer and consumer teams.







Start Simple, Iterate Begin with critical datasets and expand as adoption grows.
Use Versioning Always version schemas to avoid breaking downstream systems.
Automate Validation Implement automated checks for schema compliance and data quality.
Centralize Documentation Maintain contracts in a shared repository or data catalog for visibility.
Define SLAs Include clear expectations for freshness, completeness, and uptime.
Communicate Changes Early Use automated alerts or Slack notifications for schema updates.
Enforce Ownership Assign clear owners for both producer and consumer sides.
Integrate with CI/CD Validate data contracts during deployment to catch issues before production.
Monitor & Audit Track compliance and log violations for continuous improvement.
Align with Governance Ensure contracts comply with regulatory and internal security standards.





Placeholder: Explain how prospect research and development teams can use data contracts to ensure donor data consistency and reliability across CRM, wealth screening tools, and analytics platforms.
One obstacle when writing profiles is getting accurate information from your own institution. If you have many internal boards, the chairs and members of those boards are always in flux, and there isn’t necessarily accurate information in the database or websites to verify the reality on the ground. In theory, it makes sense to have board memberships strictly tracked in your CRM. But like with anything, it all depends on the people responsible for maintaining and providing the data in the first place.
Old or inacurrate information in your internal systems is a constant battle, and there may be some areas where you know the information tends to be more inaccurate than others, a “quirk of the system” that you’ve just learned to deal with, and one where a fix is not necessarily going to happen soon.
However, if you are ever in the position to try and fix some of these data problems, at a director-level, you can advocate for creating internal data contracts specifically to benefit your prospect development and research teams.
With the board membership example, a strong internal data contract that provides accurate, timely board information means that profiles get done faster, obvious mistakes are avoided, and researchers can spend less time spinning their wheels trying to find information that the institution already knows. Ideally, the goal of a prospect research team should be to get the most efficient process possible for highly accurate profiles, so there is more time for proactive research and expanding the prospect pool. The amount of money that is lost every year is staggering: any given team is being paid for their time, and expanding the prospect pool actually increases campaign revenues, while fiddling around with verifications can run counter to the overall momentum needed to meet fundraising goals.
Of course, if you aren’t tracking your efficiency, then it would be hard to know for sure.

title: Prospect Research Toolkit
text:
…



Howdy, partner. Welcome to camp!
You know how they say, “there’s gold in them thar hills?” - well, I’ve set out to create the resource I always wish existed: a free, community-driven library of tools for the scrappy fundraising prospector, along with a field guide to put all of them into practice.
Hopefully this toolkit will expand to be an invaluable resource for those splashing through the data streams, Excel sluice boxes and CRM picks a’clankin’! In the meantime, it’s just a hobby project with big dreams.
For more info about the scope of this project, feel free to check out the About page. Some unpolished Field Notes are available, but the tool comparison dashboard and other more interesting things are still in development.
Thanks for reading and happy trails.


Version: 0.1.2-alpha



 Back to top

title: Free External Resources
text:
The Prospect Research Institute has free resources available, but some of them are out of date, since their focus is currently on creating paid training with clients. See some research specific pages here: Prospect Research.





 Back to top

title: Nexis Uni
text:
Overview
One of the many LexisNexis variants (product page link), alongside Nexis for Development Professionals and Dilligence+, which may be more familiar to prospect researchers.
Nexis Uni is an affiliate database scoped for college-level research and emphasizes the breadth of its legal sources, making it popular with law libraries. However, given it’s 15,000+ sources, there are a variety of categories, including business.


Features


Obstacles
Unlike Nexis for Development Professionals, you cannot look up the personal info of specific people. It’s a good choice, otherwise students may be looking up other students, or professors. It may be drawing from many of the same sources as ProQuest for its news articles, though how they stack up against each other has not yet been explored in this toolkit.


Conclusion
Nexis Uni is a good choice if you are a small shop or individual researcher with access to a public library with a subscription. However, if your team has the resources to pay for Nexis for Development Professionals, it has all of the features of Nexis Uni plus ones more relevant to writing bios, confirming company information, etc.



Latest Export Info










 Back to top
